Plant Description
I. Cassava Woody shrub

III. Malabar Spinach Hebaceous climber
IV. Mint Annual shrub
V. Papaya Woody shrub
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: Consider the following pairs: Plant Description I. Cassava Woody shrub II. Ginger Herb with pseudostem III. Malabar Spinach Hebaceous climber IV. Mint Annual shrub V. Papaya Woody shrub How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Pair I is correctly matched**: Cassava is a perennial woody shrub with an edible root, which grows in tropical and subtropical areas of the world. It is also called yuca, manioc, and mandioca. It is cultivated for its starchy**tuberous **roots**, which are a major source of carbohydrates.**Pair II is correctly matched**: Ginger is an herbaceous perennial plant with a unique structure that includes a rhizome (underground stem) and a pseudostem (false stem). The rhizome, the fleshy underground stem, is the part we commonly consume. The pseudostem, made up of tightly wrapped leaf bases, supports the aerial leaves and flowers. This plant is a member of the Zingiberaceae family, which also includes plants like turmeric and cardamom.**Pair III is correctly matched: **Malabar spinach (Basella alba) is an herbaceous climber. It is a fast-growing, soft-stemmed, twining vine that can grow up to 10 feet long. It is also known as vine spinach, Indian spinach, and Ceylon spinach.**Pair IV is incorrectly matched**: Mint is not a shrub. It is classified as an aromatic perennial herb. Shrubs are typically woody plants with multiple stems, while herbs have softer, non-woody stems. Mint plants have soft, square stems and spread via underground and overground runners.**Pair V is incorrectly matched**: The papaya (Carica papaya) is a large, herbaceous perennial,**not a woody shrub**. It has a single, non-woody trunk that is somewhat succulent and soft-wooded. The trunk does not develop true bark and is marked by scars from old leaf bases. The papaya is a tree-like plant, but its trunk is not as woody as a typical tree.

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: Which of the following crops help in restoring soil fertility by fixing the atmospheric nitrogen in the soil?
I. Groundnut
II. Masur (Lentil)
III. Jowar
IV. Urad
V. Ragi
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following crops help in restoring soil fertility by fixing the atmospheric nitrogen in the soil? I. Groundnut II. Masur (Lentil) III. Jowar IV. Urad V. Ragi Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Growing a leguminous crop with cereal crops helps improve soil fertility by fixing the atmospheric nitrogen in the soil. The fixed nitrogen helps the cereal crops as well.** Option I is correct: Groundnut **is also known as earthnut, peanut, monkey nut, belongs to the family Fabaceae (**Leguminosae**). Its inclusion in crop rotation builds up soil fertility by fixing atmospheric nitrogen through the root nodules and serves as an efficient cover crop for reducing soil erosion and increasing water conservation.**Options II and IV are correct: **Pulses get over 60 percent of their nitrogen from the air. This nitrogen is then fed into the soil and allows these legumes to share this nitrogen with nearby crops, essentially reducing the need for chemical fertilizers. This unique ability is called biological nitrogen fixation – where they convert atmospheric nitrogen gas into ammonia (a plant-usable form of nitrogen). - Major pulses that are grown in India are**tur, urad, moong, masur, peas and gram.**Pulses need less moisture and survive even in dry conditions. Being leguminous crops,**all these crops help in restoring soil fertility by fixing **nitrogen from the air. **Therefore, these are mostly grown in rotation with other crops.**Options III and V are incorrect: Jowar (sorghum) and ragi (finger millet) **are non-leguminous, nutrient-intensive coarse grains. Unlike legumes, they**do not possess root nodules to fix atmospheric nitrogen. **Instead, they are typically rotated with leguminous crops to manage soil nitrogen levels, as leguminous plants are key to restoring soil fertility.

**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

It can be grown on a variety of soils and needs manual labour from sowing to harvesting.**India is the **second largest producer of sugarcane only after Brazil.**It is the main source of sugar, gur (jaggary), khandsari and molasses. The major sugarcane-producing states are Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Bihar, Punjab and Haryana.**

Option b is incorrect: Cotton grows well in drier parts**of the black cotton soil of the Deccan plateau. It**requires high temperature, light **rainfall or irrigation, 210 frost-free days **and bright sun-shines for its growth. It is a kharif crop and requires 6 to 8 months to mature. India is the second largest producer of cotton after China.**

Option c is incorrect: Soyabean**is one of the main oil-seeds produced in India. Soyabean grows well in warm and moist climates. It is grown in areas receiving annual rainfall of 40-75 cm. Well drained and fertile loam soils with a pH between 6.0 and 7.5 are most suitable for the cultivation of soybean. Water logging is injurious to the crop. It possesses a very high nutritional value. India ranks fifth in its global production.**

Option d is incorrect: Rubber is an equatorial crop, **but under special conditions, it is also grown in tropical and sub-tropical areas. It requires a**moist and humid climate with rainfall of more than 200 cm **and temperature above 25°C. It is mainly grown in Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Karnataka and Andaman and Nicobar islands andGaro hills of Meghalaya. India is the third largest producer and fourth largest consumer of natural rubber

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The shifting cultivation is a traditional agricultural practice in many parts of Asia, Africa and South America. In shifting cultivation, parts of the forest are cut and burnt in rotation. Seeds are sown in the ashes after the first monsoon rains, and the crop is harvested by October-November. Such plots are cultivated for a couple of years and then left fallow for 12 to 18 years for the forest to grow back.**It has many local names in various countries.**It is known as ‘Milpa’ in Mexico and Central America, ‘Conuco’ in Venzuela, ‘Roca’ in Brazil, ‘Masole’ in Central Africa, ‘Ladang’ in Indonesia, ‘Ray’ in Vietnam.**In India, this primitive form of cultivation**is called**‘Valre’ or ‘Waltre’ in South-eastern Rajasthan,**‘Kuruwa’ in **Jharkhand**, ‘Bewar’ or ‘Dahiya’ in Madhya Pradesh, ‘Podu’ or ‘Penda’ in Andhra Pradesh, ‘Pama Dabi’ or ‘Koman’ or Bringa’ in Odisha, ‘Kumari’ in Western Ghats, Khil’ in the Himalayan belt and ‘Jhumming’ in the North-eastern region. Among the North Eastern States in India, it is jhumming in north-eastern states like Assam, Meghalaya, Mizoram and Nagaland;**Pamlou in Manipur**, Dipa in Bastar district of Chhattishgarh, and in Andaman and Nicobar Islands. Knowledge Base:**Shifting cultivation practices have many local names in various countries.**It is known as ‘**Milpa’ in Mexico**and**Central America**, ‘**Conuco’ in Venezuela,**‘**Roca’ in Brazil**, ‘**Masole’ in Central Africa**, ‘**Ladang’ in Indonesia**, ‘**Ray’ in **Vietnam**.

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: With reference to agriculture in India, the term “barani” refers to?
questions.questionText: With reference to agriculture in India, the term “barani” refers to?

options.a: Rainfed agricultural practices in areas where irrigation facilities are not available.
options.b: A high-yielding indigenous wheat variety developed for dryland regions.
options.c: An indigenous bovine breed native to eastern India.
options.d: Agricultural wasteland left uncultivated for more than five years.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **In India, on the basis of the main source of moisture for crops, the farming can be classified as**irrigated**and**rainfed**(barani).**Barani agriculture**refers to rainfed farming**where irrigation facilities **like tube, canal, river, lakes**are not **available. **Rainfed farming is further**classified on the basis of adequacy of soil moisture**during cropping season into**dryland **and**wetland **farming. In India, the**dryland farming **is largely confined to the regions having annual** rainfall less than 75 cm.**These regions grow hardy and drought resistant crops such as ragi, bajra, moong, gram and guar (fodder crops) and practise various measures of soil moisture conservation and rain water harvesting. In**wetland farming**, the**rainfall is in excess of soil moisture requirements of plants **during the rainy season. Such regions may face flood and soil erosion hazards. These areas grow various water intensive crops such as rice, jute and sugarcane and practise aquaculture in the fresh water bodies.

statementLines: Which of the following statements with regard to Urea in India are correct?
I. Urea is the only fertilizer whose sale price is statutorily controlled by the government.
II. Neem coating of urea slows its dissolution in soil and improves nutrient uptake efficiency.
III. India’s domestic urea production grew by more than 40 percent between 2011–12 and 2023–24.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements with regard to Urea in India are correct? I. Urea is the only fertilizer whose sale price is statutorily controlled by the government. II. Neem coating of urea slows its dissolution in soil and improves nutrient uptake efficiency. III. India’s domestic urea production grew by more than 40 percent between 2011–12 and 2023–24. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. Urea is a key nitrogenous fertiliser in India. It plays a central role in agricultural productivity. Its regulation and subsidy structure differ significantly from other fertilisers.**

Statement I is correct**.**Urea is sold at a government-notified statutory price. It is the only controlled fertiliser in **India**. In contrast, Phosphatic and Potassic (P&K) fertilisers are decontrolled. They are sold at indicative Maximum Retail Prices (MRPs).**

Statement II is correct**.**Neem coating delays the release of nitrogen from urea into the soil**. Because of slower dissolution plants absorb nitrogen gradually as per need. Also, nutrient losses due to volatilisation and leaching are reduced. And crop yield improves due to better nitrogen use efficiency.**

Statement III is correct**. Between**2011-12**and**2023-24**(April-March), India’s domestic urea production has risen from 22 million tonnes to 31.4 million tonnes (mt). This reflects a**growth of over 40 percent** during the period.

1. Avoiding the monoculture practices

3. Avoiding the cultivation of plantation crops
4. Using crop residues to cover soil surface
5. Adopting spatial and temporal crop sequencing/crop rotations
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to the circumstances in Indian agriculture, the concept of "Conservation Agriculture" assumes significance. Which of the following fall under the Conservation Agriculture? 1. Avoiding the monoculture practices 2. Adopting minimum tillage 3. Avoiding the cultivation of plantation crops 4. Using crop residues to cover soil surface 5. Adopting spatial and temporal crop sequencing/crop rotations Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Conservation Agriculture (CA) is a farming system that can prevent losses of arable land while regenerating degraded lands.**Statements 2, 4 and 5 are correct:**Conservation Agriculture (CA) is a farming system that can prevent losses of arable land while regenerating degraded lands. It**promotes maintenance of a permanent soil cover, minimum soil **disturbance, and diversification of plant species.**It includes the practice related to**species diversification through **varied crop sequences and associations involving at least three different crops i.e., avoiding adopting crop **sequencing or crop rotation with respect to time and space. **It enhances biodiversity and natural biological processes above and below the ground surface, which contribute to increased water and nutrient use efficiency and to improved and sustained crop production.**Statements 1 and 3 are incorrect: **Conservation Agriculture is simply based on three core principles: minimum soil disturbance, maintenance of permanent soil cover and use of crop rotations with a diversity of crop species. It does not avoid the monoculture practices and cultivation of plantation crops.

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: With reference to land reforms in post-independence India, consider the following statements:
I. The tenancy abolition and regulation act of all the major land reforms passed in India was the most effective as it was uniformly implemented in all the states in India.
II. The ceiling limits on agricultural land were not uniform across the country and varied from State to State depending on local conditions.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to land reforms in post-independence India, consider the following statements: I. The tenancy abolition and regulation act of all the major land reforms passed in India was the most effective as it was uniformly implemented in all the states in India. II. The ceiling limits on agricultural land were not uniform across the country and varied from State to State depending on local conditions. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Land reforms in post-independence**India refer to a series of legislative and policy measures initiated after 1947 to restructure the agrarian system, remove feudal elements, and promote social justice in rural areas. The**major objectives were: **
- Abolition of intermediaries**(Zamindari, Jagirdari systems) to establish direct relations between the State and cultivators.**
- Tenancy reforms**to regulate rents and provide security of tenure.**
- Imposition of ceilings on landholdings**and redistribution of surplus land**
- Consolidation of fragmented holdings**to improve agricultural efficiency. These reforms aimed at**reducing rural inequality, empowering small and marginal farmers**, and**enhancing **agricultural productivity**, though their success varied significantly across States due to differences in implementation.**

Statement I is incorrect: **The**abolition of intermediaries**(primarily through Zamindari Abolition Acts passed by various states in the early 1950s) is**widely regarded as the most successful component of land reforms in post
- **independence India.**It eliminated layers of intermediaries like zamindars, jagirdars, etc., who collected rent but did not cultivate the land. The**tenancy abolition and regulation act**attempted either to outlaw tenancy altogether or to regulate rents to give some security to the tenants. In**most of the states, these laws were never implemented very effectively.**

Statement II is correct: **The**ceiling on landholdings**was another**major land reform**measure aimed at**reducing **concentration of land**and**redistributing surplus land to the landless**. However, since**land is a State subject **under the Indian Constitution (Seventh Schedule),**ceiling laws were enacted **by individual States**. Therefore, the**ceiling limits were not uniform across the country and varied based on factors such as: **Type and productivity of land, irrigation status, local socio-economic conditions, etc.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook/pdf/lesy204.pdf (Page 47) Forum IAS Agriculture Redbook, Chapter: 2 - Agricultural inputs, Page: 12. https://egyankosh.ac.in/bitstream/123456789/31754/1/Unit-2.pdf Page: 28

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: In the context of agricultural markets, the term ‘Cobweb Cycle’ is best associated with: years. production periods.
questions.questionText: In the context of agricultural markets, the term ‘Cobweb Cycle’ is best associated with: years. production periods.

options.a: Cyclical fluctuations in commodity prices due to changes in transportation and storage capacity.
options.b: Cyclical fluctuations in agricultural prices caused by speculative hoarding in regulated markets.
options.c: Cyclical fluctuations in agricultural procurement levels resulting from alternating surplus and deficit production
options.d: Cyclical fluctuations in agricultural prices as the quantity supplied by producers depends on prices in previous

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The**Cobweb Cycle**is an**economic phenomenon observed primarily in agricultural markets**where there is**a time lag **between production decisions and actual supply in the market.**Farmers decide how much to produce based on current market prices. However, agricultural production takes time (one cropping season or more). As a result: - High prices in the current season encourage farmers to increase production. - When the increased supply reaches the market in the next season, it leads to excess supply and a fall in prices. - The fall in prices then discourages production in the following season, causing reduced supply and a rise in prices again. This** repeated adjustment between price expectations and delayed supply response leads to cyclical oscillations in **prices and output**, forming a pattern resembling a**cobweb**when plotted graphically. Thus, the**Cobweb Cycle explains how naive price expectations combined with production lag can generate **recurring fluctuations in agricultural markets. **

It is involved in production and marketing of quality seeds, planting materials and sale of Bio-Fertilizer.**

Statement I is correct:**The**National Seeds Corporation Limited**(NSC) is a Schedule 'B' Miniratna Category-I**Central **Public Sector Undertaking**(PSU)**, wholly owned by the Government of India.**It functions under the administrative control of the**Department of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare,**which is part of the**Ministry of Agriculture and **Farmers’ Welfare. **

Statement II is incorrect: **The**NSC is not the nodal body responsible for organizing the annual National Seed **Congress. **The National Seed Congress is a premier annual event in the seed sector,**organized primarily by the **Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers’ Welfare**, Government of India.
It serves as a common platform for scientists, seed producers, and policymakers to discuss the latest developments and challenges in the seed sector.

questions.questionText: With reference to the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers’ Rights Act, 2001, consider the following statements: I. The Act grants farmers the right to save, use, sow, resow, exchange, share or sell their farm produce including branded seed of a variety protected under the act. II. Researchers can use the registered varieties under the Act for conducting experiment or research. III. The Act establishes a National Gene Fund to support benefit-sharing and conservation and sustainable use of genetic resources. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The**Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers’ Rights Act, 2001**(PPV&FR Act) is an Indian legislation enacted to**provide intellectual property protection to plant breeders**while simultaneously**safeguarding the rights of farmers**. This Act is in conformity with the International** Union for the Protection of New Varieties of Plants (UPOV), 1978. **It was enacted to comply with**India’s obligations under the WTO–TRIPS Agreement**, which mandates protection of plant varieties through patents or an effective sui generis system**. India adopted a unique sui generis model that **balances: **
- Breeders’ rights**(for development of new varieties)**
- Farmers’ rights**(to save, use, sow, resow, exchange, share or sell their farm produce)**
- Researchers’ rights**(for experimentation and research use)**

Statement I is incorrect:**Under the Protection of Plant Varieties and Farmers’ Rights Act, 2001,**farmers**have the**right to save, use, sow, resow, exchange, share**or**sell their farm produce**including seed of a variety protected**under the PPV&FR Act, 2001 However, they**cannot sell branded seed of a variety protected under the PPV&FRAct, 2001**. Sale in branded form is treated as commercial marketing, which is the**exclusive right of the breeder. **

Statement II is correct:**The**Act explicitly provides for researchers’ rights**.**Any registered variety can be used by a **researcher for conducting experiments or research purposes**. This includes the use of a variety as an initial source of variety for the purpose of developing another variety. However,**repeated use of such a variety**as a parental line for commercial production of a new variety**requires prior **authorization of the registered breeder.**

Statement III is correct: **The**Act provides for the establishment of a National Gene Fund**, and clearly lays down its specific utilization. The**Gene Fund is used for: **
- **Payment of any amount determined as**benefit sharing.**
- **Compensation payable to individual farmers or communities of farmers.**
- **Supporting**conservation and sustainable use of genetic resources**, including in-situ and ex-situ conservation, and for strengthening the capacity of Panchayats in carrying out such activities.**
- **Meeting expenditure related to schemes connected with benefit sharing.

statementLines: With reference to agricultural revolutions in India, consider the following information:
Agricultural Revolution Objective Person associated/Leader
I. Green Revolution Rapidly increase food grain production, specifically wheat and rice
M. S. Swaminathan
II. White Revolution Increase in milk production Verghese Kurien
III. Brown Revolution 1.0 Increase in leather & coffee production Hiralal Chaudhary In
how many of the above rows is the information correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to agricultural revolutions in India, consider the following information: Agricultural Revolution Objective Person associated/Leader I. Green Revolution Rapidly increase food grain production, specifically wheat and rice M. S. Swaminathan II. White Revolution Increase in milk production Verghese Kurien III. Brown Revolution 1.0 Increase in leather & coffee production Hiralal Chaudhary In how many of the above rows is the information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Information in row I is correct: Green Revolution **The Green Revolution aimed at**rapidly increasing food grain production in India, particularly wheat and rice,**through the introduction of High Yielding Variety (HYV) seeds, expansion of irrigation, chemical fertilizers, and modern agricultural techniques. M**. S. Swaminathan**played a pivotal role in adapting and promoting HYV seeds in Indian conditions and is widely regarded as the “**Father of the Green Revolution in India.” **Information in row II is correct: White Revolution **The White Revolution focused on increasing milk production and building a nationwide dairy cooperative network under Operation Flood. It transformed India from a milk-deficient country into the world’s largest milk producer contributing 32% of the global milk supply.**It was led by Verghese Kurien**, widely known** as the “Father of the White Revolution in India.” **White Revolution 2.0 was formally launched in December 2024 to strengthen dairy cooperatives, expand **employment opportunities, and increase women participation in organized dairying. **Information in row III is correct: Brown Revolution **The**Brown Revolution**(the "first" or original Brown Revolution)**aimed at promoting leather production **(from livestock) and**coffee cultivation**, particularly in tribal areas like Visakhapatnam (Andhra Pradesh).This revolution was**initiated and led by Hiralal Chaudhary. **Brown Revolution 2.0 is related to restoring soil health and fertility by converting agricultural waste into compost **and biochar, reducing pollution and boosting rural incomes. **

statementLines: With reference to the e-NAM (National Agriculture Market), consider the following statements:
I. The Small Farmers Agribusiness Consortium (SFAC) is the lead agency for implementing eNAM.
II. Participation in e-NAM is mandatory for all Agricultural Produce Market Committees (APMCs) across India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the e-NAM (National Agriculture Market), consider the following statements: I. The Small Farmers Agribusiness Consortium (SFAC) is the lead agency for implementing eNAM. II. Participation in e-NAM is mandatory for all Agricultural Produce Market Committees (APMCs) across India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **e-NAM**(National Agriculture Market) is a**pan-India electronic trading portal**launched in April 2016 by the Prime Minister of India. It is designed to**integrate existing APMC**(Agricultural Produce Market Committee) mandis across the country into a**unified online platform**, enabling “**One Nation One Market**” for agricultural commodities.**

Statement I is correct:**The**Small Farmers' Agribusiness Consortium**(SFAC) functions as the**Lead Implementing **Agency**(LIA)**for e-NAM under the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare.**It is responsible for implementation, coordination with States/UTs, capacity building, and overall monitoring of the e-NAM platform.**

Statement II is incorrect:**Participation in e-NAM is not mandatory for all Agricultural Produce Market **Committees (APMCs) across India**. It is a**voluntary, reform-linked scheme**. States/APMCs must undertake specific reforms (e.g., single trading license, single point mandi fee levy, provision for electronic trading) to integrate mandis with the platform. Agricultural marketing is a State subject, and integration with e-NAM depends on the willingness of States and the necessary reforms in their APMC Acts.

statementLines: Consider the following crops:

II. Nigerseed
III. Muskmelon

How many of the above are primarily Kharif crops in India?
questions.questionText: Consider the following crops: I. Safflower II. Nigerseed III. Muskmelon IV. Soyabean V. Jowar How many of the above are primarily Kharif crops in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **India has various food and non-food crops which are cultivated in three main cropping seasons which are Rabi, Kharif and Zaid.**Options II, IV and V are correct:**Kharif crops **are grown with the onset of monsoon in different parts of the country and these are harvested in September-October. Important crops grown during this season are paddy, maize,**jowar, **bajra, ragi, tur (arhar), moong, urad, cotton, jute, groundnut,**soyabean,**sunflower, sesamum and**nigerseed. ** Option I is incorrect:**Rabi crops **are sown in winter from October to December and harvested in summer from April to June. Some of the important rabi crops are wheat, barley, peas, gram, mustard, masur (lentil), rapeseed and**safflower. ** Option III is incorrect:**In between the rabi and the kharif seasons, there is a short season during the summer months known as the**Zaid season.**Some of the crops produced during ‘zaid’ are watermelon,**muskmelon, **cucumber, vegetables such as bitter gourd, pumpkin, ridged gourd and fodder crops. Sugarcane takes almost a year to grow.

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding the Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme:
I. Under the scheme, fertilizers fortified with micronutrients are eligible for additional subsidy.
II. The subsidies under the scheme applies to Phosphatic and Potassic fertilizers such as Di-Ammonium Phosphate
(DAP) and Muriate of Potash (MOP).
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding the Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme: I. Under the scheme, fertilizers fortified with micronutrients are eligible for additional subsidy. II. The subsidies under the scheme applies to Phosphatic and Potassic fertilizers such as Di-Ammonium Phosphate (DAP) and Muriate of Potash (MOP). Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. The Government of India introduced the**Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme on 1 April 2010**for phosphatic and potassic fertilizers. Under this scheme, a**fixed subsidy is provided for subsidised P and K Fertilizers**, including di-ammonium phosphate, based on their nutrient content.**Statement I correct**. In order to overcome the deficiency of secondary and micronutrients in soil and to encourage their application along with primary nutrients,**fertilizers that are fortified with secondary and micronutrients **receive additional subsidy. This encourages the use of nutrient-enriched fertilizers and supports balanced soil **health**.**

Statement II is correct**. The Nutrient Based Subsidy (NBS) scheme covers**Phosphatic and Potassic fertilizers**. These include:**
- Di-Ammonium Phosphate (DAP)**– a major phosphatic fertilizer.**
- Muriate of Potash (MOP)**– a key potassic fertilizer.**
- NPK complexes**– fertilizers containing Nitrogen, Phosphorus and Potassium in different proportions. Under NBS, subsidy is calculated based on the**nutrient content**of these fertilizers. The government announces a per kilogram subsidy for nutrients like Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K) and Sulphur (S). The final subsidy amount depends on how much of these nutrients the fertilizer contains.

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: Consider the following parameters:
I. Electrical Conductivity
II. Organic Carbon
III. Zinc
IV. Sand–Silt–Clay composition
V. Boron The Soil Health card under the Soil Health Card Scheme (now merged in Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (RKVY)) covers
how many of the above parameters?
questions.questionText: Consider the following parameters: I. Electrical Conductivity II. Organic Carbon III. Zinc IV. Sand–Silt–Clay composition V. Boron The Soil Health card under the Soil Health Card Scheme (now merged in Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (RKVY)) covers how many of the above parameters?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. The**Soil Health Card (SHC) Scheme**was launched in**2015**by the Government of India. It is implemented by the**Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare**. The objective is to: 1. Reduce excessive and imbalanced fertilizer use. 2. Improve soil fertility and productivity. 3. Promote sustainable agriculture. 4. Increase farmers’ income through better nutrient management. The Soil Health Card (SHC) Scheme evaluates soil fertility by testing**12 parameters**. These include macronutrients, secondary nutrients, micronutrients and certain chemical indicators.** Option I is correct**.**Electrical Conductivity (EC)**is tested under the Soil Health Card Scheme. It measures the soluble salt content of soil. High EC indicates salinity problems. Saline soils affect water absorption by plants.** Option II is correct**.**Organic Carbon**indicates the amount of organic matter present in soil. It reflects soil fertility and microbial activity. Higher organic carbon improves soil structure and nutrient retention. Hence, it is an important parameter under SHC.** Option III is correct**.**Zinc is a micronutrient**. Its deficiency is common in Indian soils. The Soil Health Card tests micronutrients such as Zinc, Iron, Copper and Manganese etc.** Option IV is incorrect**.**Soil texture refers to the percentage of sand, silt and clay in soil**. While texture influences water retention and drainage, it is**not part of the standard 12 parameters**tested under the Soil Health Card Scheme. The scheme focuses mainly on nutrient and chemical status.** Option V is correct**.**Boron**is included under the SHC parameter. It is an essential micronutrient required for plant growth and reproductive development. Knowledge Base: The Soil Health Card (SHC) Scheme evaluates soil fertility by testing**12 parameters**which include: - 3 Macronutrients – Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), Potassium (K) - 1 Secondary nutrient – Sulphur (S) - 5 Micronutrients – Zinc (Zn), Iron (Fe), Copper (Cu), Manganese (Mn), Boron (B) - 3 Other parameters – pH, Electrical Conductivity (EC), and Organic Carbon (OC) Soil Health Card scheme has been merged in Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (RKVY) scheme as one of its components under the name ‘Soil Health & Fertility’ from the year**2022-23**.

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: How is permaculture farming different from conventional chemical farming?
Permaculture farming discourages monocultural practices but in conventional chemical farming, monoculture practices are pre-dominant.
Conventional chemical farming can cause increase in soil salinity, but the occurrence of such phenomenon is not observed in permaculture farming.
Conventional chemical farming is easily possible in semi-arid regions, but permaculture farming is not so easily possible in such regions.
Practice of mulching is very important in permaculture farming but not necessarily so in conventional chemical farming.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: How is permaculture farming different from conventional chemical farming? 1. Permaculture farming discourages monocultural practices but in conventional chemical farming, monoculture practices are pre-dominant. 2. Conventional chemical farming can cause increase in soil salinity, but the occurrence of such phenomenon is not observed in permaculture farming. 3. Conventional chemical farming is easily possible in semi-arid regions, but permaculture farming is not so easily possible in such regions. 4. Practice of mulching is very important in permaculture farming but not necessarily so in conventional chemical farming. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**

Statement 1 is correct: Permaculture farming emphasizes polyculture and biodiversity**, where a variety of crops complement and support each other, enhancing soil health and pest resistance.**In contrast, conventional chemical **farming often relies on monocultures**for streamlined management and high-yield production, leading to reduced ecological balance and increased susceptibility to pests and diseases.**

Statement 2 is correct: Permaculture farming’s holistic approach, including diverse crop rotations, minimal soil **disruption, and natural nutrient cycling, helps maintain soil structure and microbial balance, preventing salinity **buildup.**Conventional chemical farming, with its excessive synthetic inputs and monoculture, disrupts these natural processes, leading to increased soil salinity over time.**

Statement 3 is incorrect: Contrary to the statement, permaculture is indeed suitable for arid and semi-arid **regions.**Permaculture principles, like water-efficient design, drought-resistant crops, and soil enhancement techniques, are adaptable to semi-arid regions, promoting sustainable farming. Conventional chemical farming may exacerbate water scarcity and soil degradation, making permaculture a viable and beneficial alternative.**

Statement 4 is correct: Mulching is a fundamental permaculture practice**, involving soil coverage with organic matter to retain moisture, moderate temperature, suppress weeds, prevent erosion, and enhance soil.**Conventional **chemical farming acknowledges mulching but may not integrate or prioritize it like permaculture does.** Synthetic inputs often replace weed control and nutrients, differing from permaculture’s holistic, regenerative approach that embraces mulching’s synergistic benefits.

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY):
I. CROPIC (Collection of Real Time Observations and Photographs of Crops) is one of the technology initiatives under the scheme.
II. All farmers, including sharecroppers and tenant farmers growing notified crops in notified areas, are eligible to enroll under the scheme on a voluntary basis.
III. The maximum premium payable by farmers is 5% for Kharif crops and 3% for rabi crops.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding the Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY): I. CROPIC (Collection of Real Time Observations and Photographs of Crops) is one of the technology initiatives under the scheme. II. All farmers, including sharecroppers and tenant farmers growing notified crops in notified areas, are eligible to enroll under the scheme on a voluntary basis. III. The maximum premium payable by farmers is 5% for Kharif crops and 3% for rabi crops. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojaa (PMFBY) is a flagship crop insurance scheme of the Government of India, designed to provide financial protection to farmers against crop loss due to natural calamities, pests and diseases. The scheme focuses on affordable premium, technology-based assessment and timely claim settlement.**

Statement I is correct**.**CROPIC (Collection of Real Time Observations and Photographs of Crops) is one of the **technology initiatives under Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY).**Under the CROPIC (Collection of Real Time Observations and Photographs of Crops) initiative, technology is used for better crop monitoring. Farmers upload geo-tagged photographs of their crops through a mobile application at different stages of crop growth. This improves transparency. It reduces subjectivity in crop cutting experiments. It strengthens real-time loss assessment.**

Statement II is correct**. As per the Revised Operational Guidelines,**all farmers growing notified crops in notified **areas are eligible.**This includes loanee farmers as well as non-loanee farmers.**Sharecroppers and tenant farmers are **also eligible**, subject to state-specific land record provisions. After the 2020 reforms in the scheme, enrolment under PMFBY has been made voluntary for all farmers.**

Statement III is incorrect**.The maximum premium payable by the farmer is 2% (not 5%)for the Kharif and oilseed crops. For rabi food and oilseeds crop it is 1.5% (not 3%) and for yearly commercial or horticultural crops it will be 5%.
The remaining premium is subsidized by the government.

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: With reference to Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) technique for rice cultivation, consider the following:
I. Reduction in methane emissions from rice fields
II. Reduction in water consumption.
III. Eliminates the need for nitrogen fertilizer through improved soil health.
IV. Improve the grain quality of rice.
How many of the above are benefits of Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) technique?
questions.questionText: With reference to Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) technique for rice cultivation, consider the following: I. Reduction in methane emissions from rice fields II. Reduction in water consumption. III. Eliminates the need for nitrogen fertilizer through improved soil health. IV. Improve the grain quality of rice. How many of the above are benefits of Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) technique?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD), developed and promoted by the International Rice Research Institute (IRRI), is one such water management technique. It allows the field to dry to a certain threshold before re-irrigation. A simple field water tube is used to monitor the water level below the soil surface.** Option I is correct**.**Methane is produced in flooded rice fields under anaerobic conditions**. AWD introduces periodic drying. This reduces the duration of anaerobic soil conditions. As a result, methane emissions decline significantly up to 30-70% compared to continuous flooding without decline in yield.** Option II is correct**. International Rice Research Institute (IRRI) findings indicate that AWD can reduce irrigation water use by approximately 15–30%. When practiced correctly, yields remain comparable to conventional flooded systems. Thus,**AWD improves water-use efficiency without yield penalty**.** Option III is incorrect**.**AWD is a water management practice**. It does not eliminate the need for nitrogen fertilizers. Nutrient management remains necessary.**While AWD may improve nutrient-use efficiency, it does not replace **fertilizer application**. Besides**AWD**has been shown to reduce nitrogen and phosphorus runoff by 30% as per some studies.** Option IV is correct**.**More aerobic regimes using AWD can effectively increase grain zinc content. **Rice is also a primary source of dietary heavy metal exposure. Aerobic conditions reduce the availability of arsenic and mercury to plants, however, an increase in cadmium uptake is also possible. Reduced irrigation inputs with AWD can also reduce the deposition of other source-water contaminants in paddy soils.

statementLines: With reference to the Participatory Guarantee Scheme (PGS) in India, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. It is a third-party certification system for organic products.
II. PGS-certification is compulsory for export of organic products.
III. The scheme is implemented by the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to the Participatory Guarantee Scheme (PGS) in India, which of the following statements is/are correct? I. It is a third-party certification system for organic products. II. PGS-certification is compulsory for export of organic products. III. The scheme is implemented by the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. The**Participatory Guarantee Scheme (PGS) is a system for certifying organic products in India**. It ensures that organic farming follows prescribed quality standards. Certification is issued in the form of a logo or documented statement.**

Statement I is incorrect**. PGS operates**outside the framework of third-party certification**. It is based on active participation of stakeholders, including farmers and consumers. It is a peer-review system rather than an external agency-based certification.**

Statement II is incorrect**.**PGS certification is primarily meant for the domestic market. It does not replace third
- **party certification required for export of organic products**. Export markets generally require internationally recognized third-party certification systems. However, products can be exported as conventional to get premium price if available.**

Statement III is correct**.**PGS is implemented by the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers’ Welfare**. It applies mainly to on-farm activities such as crop production, livestock rearing and processing and off-farm processing of their direct products. However, off-farm activities like storage and transport are not covered.

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: With reference to the Kisan Credit Card (KCC) Scheme, which of the following statements are correct?
I. RBI has recently allowed Payment banks as an eligible lending institution under the scheme.
II. The farmers eligible under the KCC scheme include Tenant farmers and sharecroppers.
III. The short-term credit support under the scheme can be utilized for purchasing heavy agricultural machinery such as tractors and combine harvesters.
IV. The short-term credit support under the scheme can be utilized for working capital for maintenance activities allied to agriculture like Bee keeping and Sericulture.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to the Kisan Credit Card (KCC) Scheme, which of the following statements are correct? I. RBI has recently allowed Payment banks as an eligible lending institution under the scheme. II. The farmers eligible under the KCC scheme include Tenant farmers and sharecroppers. III. The short-term credit support under the scheme can be utilized for purchasing heavy agricultural machinery such as tractors and combine harvesters. IV. The short-term credit support under the scheme can be utilized for working capital for maintenance activities allied to agriculture like Bee keeping and Sericulture. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. The**Kisan Credit Card (KCC) Scheme was introduced in 1998**to provide timely and affordable credit to farmers. It aims to reduce dependence on informal moneylenders and ensure smooth access to institutional finance.The scheme covers short-term credit needs for crop cultivation. Over time, its scope has been expanded to include allied activities such as animal husbandry and fisheries. The credit is provided through banks at concessional interest rates, with interest subvention benefits for prompt repayment.**

Statement I is correct**. The Kisan Credit Card Scheme is implemented by Commercial Banks, RRBs, Small Finance Banks and Cooperatives. The Eligible lending institutions under the scheme are: - Public Sector Banks (PSBs), - Private Sector Scheduled commercial Banks, - Small Finance Banks (SFBs), - Computerized Primary Agricultural Credit Societies (PACS) ceded with Scheduled Commercial Banks (SCBs), - Regional Rural Banks (RRBs) - Rural Cooperative Banks (RCBs) viz. State Cooperative Banks & District Central Cooperative Banks (DCCBs)**Payment Banks is not one of the eligible lending institution.**

Statement II is correct**.**KCC eligibility**includes owner cultivators,**tenant farmers**, oral lessees,**sharecroppers**, and Self Help Groups (SHGs) or Joint Liability Groups (JLGs) of farmers. Credit limits are calculated based on cropping patterns and purpose.**

Statement III is incorrect and IV is correct**. Kisan Credit Card (KCC) scheme meets the financial requirements of farmers at various stages of farming. The short term credit is: - To meet the short-term credit requirements for cultivation of crops; - Post-harvest expenses; - Produce marketing loan; - Consumption requirements of farmer household; -**Working capital for maintenance of farm assets and activities allied to agriculture**; - Investment credit requirement for agriculture and allied activities.**KCC is primarily designed for short-term credit needs**. Purchase of heavy machinery generally falls under medium-or long-term agricultural loans. Hence, it is not the purpose of the KCC scheme. The short time credit under the scheme can be utilized for Working capital for maintenance activities allied to agriculture Dairy, Fisheries, Poultry, other Ruminants, Mushroom,**Bee keeping **and**Sericulture **etc.

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding Drip Irrigation system:
I. In this system, water is applied drop-by-drop on the soil surface or sub-surface at a rate much higher than the infiltration rate of the soil.
II. It is suitable for almost all orchard crops, plantation crops and most of the row crops.
III. In India a Centrally Sponsored Scheme, Per Drop More Crop (PDMC) which is implemented under Pradhan Mantri
Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana focuses on enhancing water use efficiency at the farm level through this system.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding Drip Irrigation system: I. In this system, water is applied drop-by-drop on the soil surface or sub-surface at a rate much higher than the infiltration rate of the soil. II. It is suitable for almost all orchard crops, plantation crops and most of the row crops. III. In India a Centrally Sponsored Scheme, Per Drop More Crop (PDMC) which is implemented under Pradhan Mantri Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana focuses on enhancing water use efficiency at the farm level through this system. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. Drip irrigation system, also known as ‘trickle irrigation system’, is a method of applying the amount of water directly to the root zones of plants through drippers or emitters at frequent intervals.**

Statement I is incorrect**. In this system, water is applied drop-by-drop or by a micro jet on the soil surface or sub-surface** at a rate lower than the infiltration rate of the soil. **In drip irrigation, water is applied frequently at low rates from a low pressure delivery system comprising of small diameter plastic pipes fitted with outlets, called emitter or drippers, directly to the land surface close to the plant.**

Statement II is correct**.**Drip irrigation is suitable for almost all orchard crops, plantation crops and most of the **row crops. **Orchard and Plantation crops like coconut, tea, coffee, cardamom, citrus, grapes, banana, papaya, mango, guava, pineapple and pomegranate; row crops like sugarcane, cotton, groundnut, sapota, strawberry and vegetable crops including tomato, potato and other widely spaced vegetable crops and flower plants.**

Statement III is correct:**Centrally Sponsored Scheme of Per Drop More Crop (PDMC) Scheme **was earlie implemented as a component of Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchayee Yojana (PMKSY) but now the scheme is being implemented under Pradhan Mantri Rashtriya Krishi Vikas Yojana (PMRKVY). The** Scheme focuses on enhancing **water use efficiency at farm level through Micro Irrigation, namely, drip** and sprinkler irrigation systems.
Till date, 96.97 lakh ha has been covered under micro irrigation through PDMC scheme, which includes 46.37 lakh ha under drip irrigation and 50.60 lakh ha under sprinkler irrigation

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: Consider the following statements about millets:
1. Millets are often referred to as climate resilient crops because they can grow on arid lands with minimal inputs and maintenance.
2. Millets are a good source of minerals, dietary fibre, antioxidants and protein.
3. Millets, including sorghum, account for less than 3% of the global grains trade.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements about millets: 1. Millets are often referred to as climate resilient crops because they can grow on arid lands with minimal inputs and maintenance. 2. Millets are a good source of minerals, dietary fibre, antioxidants and protein. 3. Millets, including sorghum, account for less than 3% of the global grains trade. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer **

Statement 1 is correct: Millets **are known for their**ability to withstand challenging environmental conditions. **They are highly resilient to drought, heat, and poor soil quality, which makes them**ideal for arid and semi-arid regions**. Millets can thrive with minimal inputs of water, fertilizers, and pesticides, unlike crops like rice and wheat that typically require more resources.**

Statement 2 is correct: Millets are a nutrient-dense food source**. They are**rich in essential minerals **such as iron, magnesium, phosphorus, and potassium. Additionally, millets contain**high amounts of dietary fiber**,**antioxidants, **and proteins, **making them beneficial for health. Their low glycemic index also makes them suitable for people with diabetes.**

Statement 3 is correct: Millets, including sorghum (Jowar), **have a relatively**small presence in the global grains **market, accounting for less than 3% of global trade**. This is mainly due to limited international demand and the dominance of more widely consumed grains like wheat, rice, and maize.

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: Consider the following statements with reference to groundnut:
I. Groundnut accounts for more than half of the total oilseeds production in India in the Financial Year 2024-25.
II. Gujarat is the largest producer of groundnut amongst all States in India in the Financial Year 2024-25.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements with reference to groundnut: I. Groundnut accounts for more than half of the total oilseeds production in India in the Financial Year 2024-25. II. Gujarat is the largest producer of groundnut amongst all States in India in the Financial Year 2024-25. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Groundnut is also known as earthnut, peanut, monkey nut, and Manilla nut. It is drought resistant once it is established. Groundnut is cultivated for its kernel which is rich in oil (45%) and protein (20%).**

Statement I is incorrect: Groundnut is a kharif crop**and is considered the “king of oilseeds”. It is available throughout the year due to two-crop cycles, harvested in March and October. As per final crop estimates for**2024-25**, released by the Ministry of Agriculture, Total Oilseeds production was 429.89 lakh tonnes with top three being: - Soybean – 152.68 lakh tonnes (Record) - Groundnut – 119.42 lakh tonnes (Record) - Rapeseed & Mustard – 126.67 lakh tonnes Among these, soybean (35.5%),**groundnut (27.78%, and not 50%), **rapeseed & mustard (29.5%) contributes to more than 90% of total oilseeds production.**

Statement II is correct: Gujarat was the largest producer of groundnut**followed by Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh in India as per Economic Survey**2025-26**. Gujarat accounts for 43.72% of the total followed by Rajasthan at 17.89% and Madhya Pradesh at 13%.

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding Laterite soils:
I. They are formed in areas receiving high rainfall with alternating wet and dry spells.
II. Such soils are non-porous in nature and have higher water-holding capacity.
III. They are generally poor in nitrogen, phosphorus and potash.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding Laterite soils: I. They are formed in areas receiving high rainfall with alternating wet and dry spells. II. Such soils are non-porous in nature and have higher water-holding capacity. III. They are generally poor in nitrogen, phosphorus and potash. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. Laterite soil develops under conditions of**high temperature and heavy rainfall**.**Intense leaching**removes bases and silica from the upper layers. As a result, the**soil becomes acidic and nutrient-deficient.**This soil is**red to reddish
- **yellow**in colour and has**pH of 5 – 6**. It is commonly found in eastern**Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Kerala, Madhya **Pradesh, Odisha, Assam and the Ratnagiri district of Maharashtra**.**

Statement I is correct**. Laterite soil develops in tropical and sub-tropical climates with**alternating wet and dry **seasons**. Heavy rainfall leads to leaching which is an essential condition for the formation of Laterite soil.**

Statement II is incorrect**. Laterite soil is**porous and well-drained**. It has** poor water-holding capacity. **This makes it less fertile unless properly managed and adds to the leaching process.**

Statement III is correct**. Due to intense leaching,**laterite soil is poor in nitrogen, phosphorus, potash, magnesium **and lime.** However, after adopting appropriate soil conservation techniques particularly in the hilly areas of Karnataka, Kerala and Tamil Nadu, this soil is very useful for growing tea and coffee. Red laterite soils in Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh and Kerala are more suitable for crops like cashewnut.

statementLines: With reference to the Food Corporation of India (FCI), consider the following:
I. Strategic buffer stock of essential food grains.
II. Development and regulation of warehouses, including negotiability of warehouse receipts.
III. Implementing Price Deficiency Payment Scheme.
How many of the above are the functions of the Food Corporation of India (FCI)?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Food Corporation of India (FCI), consider the following: I. Strategic buffer stock of essential food grains. II. Development and regulation of warehouses, including negotiability of warehouse receipts. III. Implementing Price Deficiency Payment Scheme. How many of the above are the functions of the Food Corporation of India (FCI)?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. The Food Corporation of India (FCI) was established under the Food Corporations Act, 1964. Its core mandate is to support India’s food security architecture. This includes procurement of foodgrains at MSP, maintenance of buffer stocks and their allocation for distribution under welfare schemes such as the National Food Security Act (NFSA).** Option I is correct**.**FCI manages the strategic buffer stock of essential food grains to meet any unforeseen **contingencies, such as droughts, floods, or other emergencies **in accordance with buffer norms fixed by the Government of India. These stocks serve three purposes: 1. Ensuring food security under NFSA and other welfare schemes. 2. Stabilising prices during shortages. 3. Meeting emergency requirements. This function is central to FCI’s statutory mandate.** Option II is incorrect**.**Electronic Negotiable Warehouse Receipts (e-NWRs**) are governed under the Warehousing (Development and Regulation) Act, 2007.**The Warehousing Development and Regulatory Authority (WDRA) was set **up to ensure implementation of the provisions of the Warehousing (Development & Regulation) Act, 2007**. The main objective of WDRA is to implement Negotiable Warehouse Receipt (NWR) System in the country, which would help farmers to store their produce in scientific storage godowns near by their farms and to seek loan from banks against their NWR. The**main functions of the Authority are to make provisions for the development and regulation of **warehouses which interalia includes negotiability of warehouse receipts**, registration of warehouses, promotion of scientific warehousing of goods, improving fiduciary trust of depositors and banks, enhancing liquidity in rural areas and promoting efficient supply chain.** Option III is incorrect**. The**Price Deficiency Payment Scheme (PDPS**) is a mechanism where farmers are compensated for the difference between MSP and market price without physical procurement. FCI’s mandate is physical procurement at MSP. It does not implement PDPS.**Department of Agriculture & Farmers’ Welfare (DA&FW) **implements the Price Deficiency Payment Scheme (PDPS). **

statementLines: Which of the following statements with regard to Natural Farming is/are correct?
I. It is an agroecology-based farming system that integrates crops, trees and livestock with functional biodiversity.
II. It relies on on-farm inputs such as Bijamrita for seed treatment and Jivamrita for enhancing soil microbial activity.
III. One of its objective is to reduce input cost of agricultural production.
IV. The National Mission on Natural Farming in India proposed in the Union Budget 2026-27 as a central sector scheme.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements with regard to Natural Farming is/are correct? I. It is an agroecology-based farming system that integrates crops, trees and livestock with functional biodiversity. II. It relies on on-farm inputs such as Bijamrita for seed treatment and Jivamrita for enhancing soil microbial activity. III. One of its objective is to reduce input cost of agricultural production. IV. The National Mission on Natural Farming in India proposed in the Union Budget 2026-27 as a central sector scheme. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. Indian agriculture has witnessed rising input costs, declining soil fertility and ecological stress due to excessive use of synthetic fertilizers and pesticides. In response, Natural Farming has emerged as a sustainable alternative. It seeks to restore soil biology, reduce external dependency and promote ecological balance.**Natural Farming works with **natural processes rather than against them**. It emphasizes biodiversity, microbial activity and local resource utilization.**

Statement I is correct**.**Natural Farming is not monocropping. It promotes diversified farming systems. Crops, **trees and livestock are integrated to create ecological balance**. Functional biodiversity improves nutrient cycling, pest control and system resilience.**

Statement II is correct**. Natural Farming relies on various on-farm inputs.**Zero Budget Natural Farming (ZBNF)**is one the most famous methods of natural farming in India.**Bijamrita**is used for seed treatment. It protects seeds from soil-borne diseases.**Jivamrita**is a fermented microbial formulation prepared from cow dung, cow urine, jaggery, pulse flour and soil. It stimulates growth of microbes and earthworms and enhances nutrient availability in soil.**

Statement III is correct**. Na**tural Farming reduces dependency on purchased fertilizers and pesticides. **This lowers the cost of cultivation. It also**promotes local input preparation**and diversified farming activities. This creates employment opportunities in rural areas.**

Statement IV is incorrect: National Mission on Natural Farming (NMNF) is a standalone Centrally Sponsored **Scheme launched in November 2024 to promote chemical-free, ecosystem-based natural farming rooted in **traditional knowledge. **

questions.subject: Agriculture
statementLines: With reference to agricultural practices, which of the following are advantages of Intercropping over
Monocropping?
I. More efficient utilization of sunlight, water and nutrients.
II. Reduced incidence of pests and diseases due to crop diversity.
III. Lower labour requirement for field management.
IV. Better weed suppression due to closer crop canopy.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to agricultural practices, which of the following are advantages of Intercropping over Monocropping? I. More efficient utilization of sunlight, water and nutrients. II. Reduced incidence of pests and diseases due to crop diversity. III. Lower labour requirement for field management. IV. Better weed suppression due to closer crop canopy. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**.**Intercropping **is an agricultural practice in which** two or more crops are grown simultaneously on the same field **during a single cropping season**in a definite row pattern or spatial arrangement. - It is adopted to**ensure better resource utilisation, yield stability, risk reduction**, and**improved soil health **compared to monocropping**. - Common examples include**cereal–legume combinations**such as maize with pigeon pea or wheat with mustard. - The practice promotes crop diversity and enhances overall farm productivity per unit area.** Option I is correct: Intercropping allows better utilization of available resources such as sunlight, water, nutrients **and space **because different crops have varying root depths, nutrient requirements and canopy structures. This complementary use of resources**increases overall productivity compared to monocropping.** Option II is correct: Crop diversity in intercropping reduces the spread of pests and diseases**. The presence of different crops interrupts pest life cycles and makes it more difficult for pests and pathogens to spread rapidly, unlike in monocropping where a single crop species is uniformly available.** Option III is incorrect: This is the primary disadvantage of intercropping**. Managing different crops, each with unique requirements for fertilizer, irrigation, and harvesting requires more intensive monitoring and manual intervention. Therefore,**it generally has a higher labour requirement for field management and is harder to mechanize.** Option IV is correct: Intercropping can suppress weeds effectively**because combined crop canopy coverage reduces the amount of sunlight reaching the soil surface, thereby limiting weed growth.

statementLines: Consider the following:

questions.questionText: Consider the following: I. Processed agricultural products such as chocolate II. Fish and fish products III. Jute and hard fibres and products How many of the above are covered under the World Trade Organization’s (WTO) Agreement on Agriculture (AoA)?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. The WTO Agreement on Agriculture (AoA), which came into force in 1995, regulates trade in agricultural products. It disciplines domestic support, export subsidies and market access. The scope of products covered and the limits on domestic support are clearly defined in the Agreement.** Option I is correct**.**World Trade Organization’s (WTO) Agreement on Agriculture (AoA)**covers basic farm goods, such as wheat, milk and live animals as well as food products derived from them, such as bread, butter and meat.**Processed agricultural products such as chocolate and sausages are also included**— as are wines, spirits and tobacco products, fibres such as cotton, wool and silk, and raw animal skins for leather production.** Option II is incorrect**.**Fish and fish products are excluded from the Agreement on Agriculture. **They are treated separately under WTO rules and are not classified as agricultural products under the AoA framework.** Option III is incorrect: Jute and hard fibres**and products**are covered under the Agreement on Textiles and Clothing,**not under the**Agreement on Agriculture**. Knowledge Base: The**Agreement on Agriculture **covers agricultural products and excludes forestry, fisheries, and fibres like jute and coir.

The list of 22 mandated agricultural crops that come under the Minimum Support Prices (MSP) regimes includes 14 Kharif crops viz. paddy, jowar, bajra, maize, ragi, tur (arhar), moong, urad, groundnut, soyabean (yellow), sunflower, sesamum, nigerseed and cotton and 6 Rabi crops viz. wheat, barley, gram, masur (lentil), rapeseed & mustard, safflower and two commercial crops viz. jute and copra.** Option I is correct**.**Wheat**is a major Rabi cereal crop, which is covered under MSP.** Option II is correct**.**Gram (chana)**is a Rabi pulse crop, which is covered under MSP.** Option III is correct**.**Rapeseed & Mustard**is a Rabi oilseed crop, which is covered under MSP.** Option IV is correct**.**Soyabean (yellow)**is a Kharif oilseed crop, which is covered under MSP.** Option V is correct**.**Nigerseed**is a Kharif oilseed crop, which is covered under MSP.

statementLines: Which of the following statements with reference to the provisions of the National Food Security Act (NFSA),
2013 are correct?
I. It legally entitles 75% of the total population of the country to receive subsidized foodgrains under the Targeted
Public Distribution System.
II. The approach to food security under the Act is primarily welfare-based in nature.
III. The eldest woman of the household of age 18 years or above is mandated to be the head of the household for the purpose of issuing of ration cards under the Act.
IV. As per the act, in case of non-supply of the entitled quantities of foodgrains to entitled persons, such persons shall be entitled to receive food security allowance.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements with reference to the provisions of the National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013 are correct? I. It legally entitles 75% of the total population of the country to receive subsidized foodgrains under the Targeted Public Distribution System. II. The approach to food security under the Act is primarily welfare-based in nature. III. The eldest woman of the household of age 18 years or above is mandated to be the head of the household for the purpose of issuing of ration cards under the Act. IV. As per the act, in case of non-supply of the entitled quantities of foodgrains to entitled persons, such persons shall be entitled to receive food security allowance. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. The National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013 converts existing food security programmes into legal entitlements. It provides subsidised foodgrains to eligible households under the Targeted Public Distribution System (TPDS).**

Statement I is incorrect**. The Act covers up**to 75% of the rural population and up to 50% of the urban population**, not 75% of the total population uniformly.**

Statement II is incorrect**. The National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013 marks a major shift in India’s food policy. Earlier, food distribution was treated largely as a welfare programme. With NFSA, food security became a**legal **entitlement**. This means eligible citizens can demand foodgrains as a matter of right. There are grievance redressal mechanisms to address various issues under the act. Thus,**the National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013 is rights
- **based**,**not merely welfare-based. **

Statement III is correct**. The Act contains a**socially progressive provision**. It states that the eldest woman, who is not less than 18 years of age, shall be treated as the head of the household for the purpose of issuing ration cards. This provision**strengthens women's agency**within households. It links food entitlement with**gender empowerment**.**

Statement IV is correct**. The Act recognises that supply failures may occur.**In case of non-supply of the entitled **quantities of foodgrains or meals to entitled persons under NFSA, such persons shall be entitled to receive such **food security allowance** from the concerned State Government to be paid to each person, within such time and manner as may be prescribed by the Central Government. These provisions are governed through the Food Security Allowance Rules, 2015. Either food must be given, or compensation must be paid. This provision reinforces the rights-based character of the Act.

questions.subject: Geography
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: In areas of intensive cultivation leading to excessive drawing of ground water, the fertile soils can gradually become saline.
Statement II: Capillary action of water leads to accumulation of dissolved salts in the upper layers of the soil over time.
Which of the above is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: In areas of intensive cultivation leading to excessive drawing of ground water, the fertile soils can gradually become saline. Statement II: Capillary action of water leads to accumulation of dissolved salts in the upper layers of the soil over time. Which of the above is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I.
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct.
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. Soil salinity is one of the major problems in regions of intensive agriculture. It is especially visible in areas where irrigation is heavily used, such as the Green Revolution belt.**Initially, these soils are fertile. However, over time, **improper irrigation practices disturb the natural salt balance of the soil**.**

Statement I is correct**.**In regions of intensive cultivation , irrigation is used frequently and in large quantities for **growing multiple crops in a year. Excessive irrigation due to intensive cultivation and poor drainage results in salt **accumulation in the soil.**Over time, this reduces soil fertility and makes the soil saline. Such soils are formed as a result of saline irrigation water and over-irrigation for a long time, which raises the water table of the soil. Thus, even fertile soils can become unproductive due to salinisation. For example in states of Punjab, Haryana, western Uttar Pradesh, over irrigation is responsible for land degradation due to water logging leading to increase in salinity.**

Statement II is correct**.**Irrigation water invariably contains small quantities of dissolved salts**. When irrigation is carried out repeatedly, salts continuously enter the soil system leading to the problem of water-logging and salinity. However,**under conditions of high temperature and evaporation, salts along with water also move upward through **capillary action toward the soil surface**. As this surface water evaporates, the dissolved salts do not evaporate with it, instead, they are left behind in the upper soil layers. Gradually, with continuous irrigation and repeated cycles of evaporation, these salts accumulate in the topsoil causing salinity.**Statement II explains Statement I**.**Excessive irrigation in intensively cultivated areas brings large quantities of **salts into the soil**. Irrigation water always contains dissolved salts. When such irrigation is repeated over time, salts continuously enter the soil system. Due to**capillary movement**, water rises towards the surface. When this water evaporates, the dissolved salts are left behind in the upper soil layer. With inadequate drainage, these salts are not removed. Gradually, the**concentration of salts increases in the topsoil**. This process converts fertile soils into saline soils.

source.sourceText: https://www.fao.org/4/x5870e/x5870e05.htm - Point 8 - Influence of Groundwater Currents and Capillary Rise;https://ncert.nic.in/textbook/pdf/jess101.pdf pg 6 https://ncert.nic.in/vocational/pdf/kegr105.pdf - Soils in India – Saline soil– Page 107; ForumIAS Red book - Agriculture - 2nd Edition – Chapter 3 – Agricultural Outputs – Agricultural Soils – Saline Soil - Page 63

statementLines: With reference to agricultural practices, consider the following statements regarding Fertigation:
I. It may increase the crop yield by 25–30 per cent.
II. It ensures uniform and precise application of nutrients as per crop requirement.
III. It reduces fertiliser use and improves nutrient uptake efficiency.
Which of the above are the advantages of Fertigation?
questions.questionText: With reference to agricultural practices, consider the following statements regarding Fertigation: I. It may increase the crop yield by 25–30 per cent. II. It ensures uniform and precise application of nutrients as per crop requirement. III. It reduces fertiliser use and improves nutrient uptake efficiency. Which of the above are the advantages of Fertigation?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**.**Fertigation is a precise, controlled and tested method of applying fertilisers, nutrients and other water-soluble **products through drip lines and sometimes by microsprinkler irrigation **systems as per crop requirements, its stage, canopy size, soil or season, etc.** Option I is correct**.** Fertigation improves crop yield by enabling timely and precise nutrient application directly to **the root zone**. This enhances nutrient availability, reduces nutrient stress, and supports optimal plant growth. Various studies report yield increases of**around 25–30 per cent**under fertigation systems.** Option II is correct**.**Fertiliser injectors regulate the exact concentration of nutrient solution. Drip emitters **distribute this solution uniformly near the root zone.**Since nutrient supply can be adjusted according to crop stage, canopy size and season, precision is achieved. This prevents underfeeding and overfeeding.** Option III is correct**. As**nutrients are delivered directly to the root zone in small, controlled doses**fertiliser savings of about 25–30% are possible. Losses due to runoff or deep percolation are reduced. Moreso, Plants absorb nutrients more efficiently.**And higher uptake efficiency leads to increased yield.**
